snippet:
@Select("SELECT lp.posting_id, lp.txn_id, lp.amount, lp.memo, " +
            "       lt.message, lt.settlement_time, lt.initiator_uuid_bin, lt.plugin_system " +
            "FROM ledger_postings lp " +
            "JOIN ledger_txns lt ON lp.txn_id = lt.txn_id " +
            "WHERE lp.account_id = #{accountId} " +
            "ORDER BY lt.settlement_time DESC, lp.posting_id DESC " +
            "LIMIT #{limit} OFFSET #{offset}")
description: The REST API's paginated account transaction history filters on ledger_postings.account_id but orders by the *joined* ledger_txns.settlement_time DESC. No index supports this: ledger_postings has idx_postings_account(account_id), idx_postings_txn(txn_id), and idx_postings_account_txn_amount(account_id,txn_id,amount) — none can provide settlement_time ordering, which lives in the joined table. Every page therefore materialises all of the account's postings, joins to ledger_txns, and filesorts, degrading as an account's history grows. The in-process Treasury LedgerMapper.findTransactionsByAccount deliberately orders by p.posting_id DESC (served index-only by idx_postings_account, with an explicit comment 'Do not revert to ORDER BY t.settlement_time') — so the two engines return reverse-chronological history via different mechanisms and the REST one is the slow, unindexed variant.
recommendation: Align the REST query with the Treasury one: ORDER BY lp.posting_id DESC (posting_id is auto-increment, monotonic with insert time, and covered by idx_postings_account so the sort is a backward index range scan with no filesort). This also removes the behavioural divergence between the two engines' history ordering.
verification_note: Snippet matches verbatim at LedgerMapper.java:60-66 — the REST query filters WHERE lp.account_id and ORDER BY lt.settlement_time DESC, lp.posting_id DESC. Index claim verified in V1__initial.sql:143-145: ledger_postings has exactly idx_postings_txn(txn_id), idx_postings_account(account_id), idx_postings_account_txn_amount(account_id,txn_id,amount); no later migration adds a posting index. None carries settlement_time (that column lives on ledger_txns), so the ORDER BY forces a full materialization of the account's postings, a join to ledger_txns, and a filesort — degrading with history size. The contrast is real: treasury/.../mappers/LedgerMapper.java:112-113,180-181 deliberately uses ORDER BY p.posting_id DESC with the exact comment 'Do not revert to ORDER BY t.settlement_time', served index-only by idx_postings_account. Both engines return reverse-chronological history but the REST one is the slow, unindexed variant. Severity minor is fair — a performance/index-coverage divergence, not a correctness or money-loss bug.

snippet:
CREATE TABLE firm_sale (
    sale_id        BIGINT       NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
    firm_id        INT          NOT NULL,
    occurred_at    DATETIME     NOT NULL,
    buyer_uuid_bin BINARY(16)   NULL,
    world          VARCHAR(32)  NOT NULL,
    x              INT          NOT NULL,
    y              INT          NOT NULL,
    z              INT          NOT NULL,
    item_id        VARCHAR(64)  NOT NULL,
    item_name      VARCHAR(128) NOT NULL,
    qty            INT          NOT NULL,
    price          DECIMAL(19,4) NOT NULL,
    source_msg_id  VARCHAR(128) NULL,
    PRIMARY KEY (sale_id),
description: firm_sale is dead schema. V6's own header states it 'supersedes business-rian's stubbed firm_sale', and chestshop_sale replaced it. A full grep across all in-scope Java and TypeScript (treasury, business, treasury-api-plugin, treasury-rest-api, chestshop, economy-explorer, excluding build/test) finds zero readers or writers of firm_sale — no mapper, no Kysely query references it. Yet V8 later added a UNIQUE constraint (uq_sale_source_msg_id) to this unused table, spending migration effort (and a documented prod-failure risk on duplicate source_msg_id) enforcing an invariant on a table nothing writes. The table and its V8 constraint persist in the reconstructed final schema as orphaned objects.
recommendation: Add a migration to DROP TABLE firm_sale (it has no inbound FKs and no code depends on it), removing both the dead table and the pointless V8 UNIQUE it carries. If retention of any legacy rows matters, archive them first; otherwise drop outright.

snippet:
@Select("SELECT d.delivery_id AS deliveryId, d.subscription_id AS subscriptionId, d.txn_id AS txnId, "
            ...
            + "WHERE d.status = 'PENDING' AND d.next_attempt_at <= NOW() "
            + "ORDER BY d.delivery_id ASC LIMIT #{limit}")
description: The webhook dispatcher's due-poll filters WHERE status='PENDING' AND next_attempt_at <= NOW() but orders by delivery_id ASC. The supporting index is idx_delivery_due(status, next_attempt_at) (V13) — it seeks the due set efficiently but does not carry delivery_id, so the ORDER BY delivery_id requires a filesort of the matched (PENDING+due) rows on every poll tick. The set is normally small (a retry outbox), so impact is bounded, but under a delivery-failure backlog the PENDING set grows and every poll re-sorts it.
recommendation: Either order by (next_attempt_at, delivery_id) so the existing idx_delivery_due leading columns satisfy the sort, or extend the index to idx_delivery_due(status, next_attempt_at, delivery_id) to make the current delivery_id ordering index-served. Confirm the dispatcher's fairness requirement (oldest-enqueued vs earliest-due) before picking.
